title: Chapter 10: Rotational Motion - Study Notes
source: A Quick Review of E-PCM for Engineering Entrance - Physics

sections.heading: Moment of Inertia (MI)
sections.content: Moment of inertia is rotational inertia: resistance against change in rotational motion. It depends on mass and mass distribution about axis.
formulas: For point mass: I = m r^2
For system: I = sum(m_i r_i^2)
For continuous body: I = integral r^2 dm
Radius of gyration: I = M k^2
specialCases: Unit: kg m^2, dimension: M L^2.
MI is larger when mass is farther from axis.
Raw egg has greater effective MI than boiled egg in spin tests.

sections.heading: Standard MI Results and Theorems
sections.content: Common body MI formulas and axis theorems are heavily used in objective problems.
formulas: Rod about centre perpendicular to length: I = M L^2/12
Rod about end perpendicular to length: I = M L^2/3
Ring about centre perpendicular to plane: I = M R^2
Disc about centre perpendicular to plane: I = M R^2/2
Solid sphere about diameter: I = 2 M R^2/5
Hollow sphere about diameter: I = 2 M R^2/3
Parallel axis theorem: I = I_cm + M h^2
Perpendicular axis theorem (lamina): I_z = I_x + I_y
specialCases: For planar lamina, perpendicular-axis theorem is valid.
For any rigid body, parallel-axis theorem always valid.

sections.heading: Rolling Motion and Energy Split
sections.content: For rolling, kinetic energy has translational and rotational parts. No slipping condition links linear and angular motion.
formulas: Pure rolling: v = omega R, a = alpha R
Total KE in rolling: K = (1/2) M v^2 + (1/2) I omega^2
With I = M k^2 and v = omega R: K_rot/K_trans = k^2/R^2
If K_rot is x% of K_trans: x/100 = k^2/R^2
specialCases: For ring, rotational share is highest among common bodies.
For rolling without slipping on incline: acceleration depends on I.

sections.heading: Rolling on Inclined Plane
sections.content: Bodies on rough incline may roll without slipping. Larger MI gives smaller acceleration and longer time.
formulas: a = g sin(theta) / (1 + I/(M R^2))
For solid sphere: a = (5/7) g sin(theta)
For disc/cylinder: a = (2/3) g sin(theta)
For ring: a = (1/2) g sin(theta)
Maximum height on incline for rolling body with speed v: h = (v^2/2g) * (1 + I/(M R^2))
specialCases: On smooth incline (no friction), rolling without slip cannot be sustained from rest.
Among sphere/disc/ring from same height: solid sphere reaches first.

sections.heading: Torque, Angular Momentum, and Power
sections.content: Torque is rotational analogue of force. Angular momentum and rotational KE govern rotational dynamics.
formulas: Torque: tau = r x F = I alpha
Angular momentum: L = r x p = I omega
Work by torque: W = integral tau dtheta
Power in rotation: P = tau omega
Rotational KE: K = (1/2) I omega^2
specialCases: If external torque is zero, angular momentum is conserved.
Skater effect: reducing I increases omega (L conserved).

sections.heading: Angular Kinematics and Conservation Applications
sections.content: Rotational motion follows kinematics analogous to linear motion; conservation laws solve many MCQs quickly.
formulas: omega = omega0 + alpha t
omega^2 = omega0^2 + 2 alpha theta
theta = omega0 t + (1/2) alpha t^2
theta = ((omega0 + omega)/2) t
Number of revolutions n = theta / (2 pi)
specialCases: Retardation problems are solved by taking alpha negative.
For uniformly changing omega, average angular speed is arithmetic mean.
